elements.sent-2411-001: We consider a two-component reaction-diffusion system that has previously been developed to model invasion of cells into a resident cell population.
elements.sent-2411-002: This system is a generalisation of the well-studied Fisher--KPP reaction diffusion equation.
elements.sent-2411-003: By explicitly calculating families of travelling wave solutions to this problem, we observe that a general initial condition with either compact support, or sufficiently large exponential decay in the far field, tends to the travelling wave solution that has the largest possible decay at its front.
elements.sent-2411-004: Initial conditions with sufficiently slow exponential decay tend to those travelling wave solutions that have the same exponential decay as their initial conditions.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also show that in the limit that the (nondimensional) resident cell death rate is large, the system has similar asymptotic structure as the Fisher--KPP model with small cut-off factor, with the same universal (leading order) logarithmic dependence on the large parameter.
elements.sent-2411-006: The asymptotic analysis in this limit explains the formation of an interstitial gap (a region preceding the invasion front in which both cell populations are small), the width of which is also logarithmically large in the cell death rate.
elements.sent-2411-007: Systems of reaction-diffusion partial differential equations (PDEs) have been used to model the interaction of species in ecology, biology and chemistry since the pioneering work of \citet{fisher1937wave and \citet{kolmogorov1937etude .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: The canonical example is the Fisher--KPP equation, which couples linear diffusion with logistic growth, and may be written (nondimensionally) as \begin{equation}\label{eq:fkpp} \pfrac{u}{t} = \pfrac{^2u}{x^2} + u(1-u).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-009: The most widely-studied property of the Fisher--KPP equation \eqref{eq:fkpp is that a localised initial condition on an unbounded spatial domain will evolve to a travelling wave solution, by which the solution transitions from the unstable state $u=0$ to the stable state $u=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-010: It is well known that, for an exponentially decaying initial condition $u \sim \e^{-ax}$, $x\to\infty$, the travelling wave (with associated wave speed $c$) that is selected is the one that has the same exponential decay rate, if such a solution exists, or the one with maximum decay rate $a=1$ otherwise \citep{aronson1975nonlinear,aronson1978multidimensional .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This selection implies that the wave speed is given by \begin{equation}c =  2 a \geq 1 \\\displaystyle a + \frac{1}{a} a < 1. \end{equation} A compactly supported initial condition (which may be thought of as the limiting case $a\to\infty$) tends to the travelling wave with minimum speed $c=2$.
elements.sent-2411-012: It was established in \citet{bramson1983convergence that, for a compactly supported initial condition, the rate at which the travelling wave speed is approached is logarithmic; if $x_f(t)$ is the spatial location of a characteristic point on the wave (for example, the point at which $u = 1/2$), then \begin{equation}x_f = 2t - \frac{3}{2}\log t + O(1), \qquad t \to \infty.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-013: As well as of inherit interest, this slow convergence is important to take into account when trying to estimate the long-time wave speed from numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-014: Many extensions to the Fisher--KPP equation \eqref{eq:fkpp have been considered, with particular focus on the resulting wave speed of travelling wave solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: A modification of the reaction term in \eqref{eq:fkpp to have a small `cut-off' was considered in \citet{brunet1997shift : \begin{equation}\pfrac{u}{t} = \pfrac{^2u}{x^2} + u(1-u)f(u;\epsilon), \qquad f(u;\epsilon) = \begin{cases} 1 u > \epsilon 0 u \leq \epsilon \end{cases}, \label{eq:fkppCutoff}\end{equation} where $\epsilon \ll 1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: The motivation behind this cut-off term comes from considering \eqref{eq:fkpp as the continuous limit of a discrete process, where $\epsilon$ represents the discrete cell size.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The matched asymptotic analysis in \cite{brunet1997shift established that the correction to the wave speed in the limit $\epsilon \to 0$ was logarithmic: \begin{equation}c \sim 2 - \frac{\pi^2}{(\log\epsilon)^2}, \qquad \epsilon \to 0, \label{eq:fkppCutoffSpeed}\end{equation} and that this correction was universal for a more general class of cut-off functions.}
elements.sent-2411-018: The matched asymptotic argument has more recently been made rigorous using geometric singular perturbation theory in \citet{dumortier2007critical .}
elements.sent-2411-019: To model more complex biological processes such as tumour growth, in which multiple cell or chemical species may be involved, systems of multiple reaction-diffusion models have been developed and analysed.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this model, the diffusivity of the invading cells is assumed to be a decreasing function of the resident cell population, such that when the healthy cell population is at capacity, the diffusivity vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-021: The decrease in resident cell population due to acidity is thus required to allow the tumour to grow.
elements.sent-2411-022: In \citet{gatenby1996reaction it was numerically observed that solutions to their three-component model tend to travelling wave solutions as time increases.}
elements.sent-2411-023: A particular point of interest was that when the death of resident cells due to acidity was large, there was a separation between the advancing front of invading cells and receding front of resident cells, in which both cell populations were small.
elements.sent-2411-024: This region is referred to as the hypocellular interstitial gap, and was observed in experimental observations.
elements.sent-2411-025: The asymptotic analysis of travelling wave solutions has also been carried out more recently in \citet{fasano2009slow,davis2022traveling ; the interstitial gap is a feature of waves that have speed $O(1)$ in the limit that acid diffusivity is much larger than the cell diffusivity (referred to as `slow' waves in \citet{fasano2009slow,davis2022traveling , the `fast' waves relating to the finite diffusivity of the acid species).}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Simplified versions of the three-species model of \citet{gatenby1996reaction have been studied, by neglecting the acid species, and directly modelling the effect of resident cell population on the diffusivity of the invading species.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The stability of travelling wave solutions was considered in \citet{swartwood2025stability , who show that all travelling waves are stable, regardless of the speed $c$ (this is similar to the Fisher--KPP equation \eqref{eq:fkpp , where the marginal stability threshold is considered as the relevant wavespeed selection mechanism \citep{van1988front,van2003front ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In that study, families of travelling wave solutions are constructed in phase space by a shooting algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-029: A minimum wave speed is identified as a function of the resident cell death rate, and the initial resident cell population.
elements.sent-2411-030: Recently, \citet{browning2019bayesian and \citet{el2021travelling consider another version of a simplified model of tumour growth whereby the two cell species react directly.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this model, both the diffusion and the carrying capacity of the invading cells is inhibited by the healthy cell population, and resident cells $v$ do not proliferate, but are only destroyed by the invading species: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[(1-v)\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right] + u(1-u-v), \label{eq:uPDE} \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = -\gamma u v \label{eq:vPDE},\end{equation} \end{subequations In this model, $u$ is the invading species, and $v$ is the resident species.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[(1-v)\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} \right] + u(1-u-v), \label{eq:uPDE} \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = -\gamma u v \label{eq:vPDE},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-032: This model may be seen as a version of the system studied in \citet{colson2021travelling with an additional effect of $v$ on the carrying capacity of $u$.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Nominally this model has a single parameter, the dimensionless resident cell death rate $\gamma$, but since in this model there is no proliferation of $v$, the initial condition of $v$ plays an important role.
elements.sent-2411-034: The diffusivity of $u$ vanishes at $v=1$, but the diffusion will only be degenerate if the initial condition takes this value.
elements.sent-2411-035: Although travelling wave solutions of \eqref{eqs:PDEs were observed in PDE simulations in \citet{el2021travelling over a range of parameter values and initial conditions, and the boundary value problem for travelling wave solutions was posed, the explicit computation of travelling wave solutions was not performed, and the mechanism by which the wave speed is determined by the parameters was not established.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: In this article we consider the model \eqref{eqs:PDEs .}
elements.sent-2411-037: We explicitly calculate travelling wave solutions via numerical continuation over a large range of parameter values (in particular the resident cell death rate $\gamma$), and compare with time-dependent numerical simulations.
elements.sent-2411-038: We are thus able to numerically observe that, although the details of the phase space are more complicated, the principle by which the wave speed is selected is the same as for the simpler Fisher--KPP system: that is, an initial condition with sufficiently small exponential decay rate will tend to the travelling wave solution with the same decay rate (if it exists), and that an initial condition with compact support (or sufficiently large exponential decay) will tend to the travelling wave solution that has maximum decay rate.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is similar to the observation made in \citet{colson2021travelling for their simpler system.}
elements.sent-2411-040: In addition, our method of calculating travelling wave solutions allows us to compute solutions in the asymptotic limit as the resident cell death rate $\gamma$ becomes large.
elements.sent-2411-041: We show that the asymptotic structure in this limit is very similar to that for the Fisher--KPP system with cut off \eqref{eq:fkppCutoff , in which $\gamma^{-1}$ plays the role of the small parameter $\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: The leading order correction to the velocity is the same as the universal correction \eqref{eq:fkppCutoffSpeed .}
elements.sent-2411-043: We also find the next term in the correction, although this requires the numerical calculation of prefactors in each of the leading order problems in the asymptotic analysis.
elements.sent-2411-044: By carrying out a matched asymptotic expansion in this limit, we observe that the wave speed converges logarithmically to the (nondimensional) Fisher--KPP wave speed of $c = 2$.
elements.sent-2411-045: This asymptotic solution also explains why the model exhibits an interstitial gap where both cell populations are small; the width of this zone is also logarithmically dependent on the cell death parameter.
elements.sent-2411-046: We show that the predictions made by the asymptotic analysis agree with the numerically computed travelling wave solutions.
elements.sent-2411-047: We start by describing the method and results of numerical simulations of the system \eqref{eqs:PDEs .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The system \eqref{eqs:PDEs comprises a population density of invading cells $u(x,t)$ invading a stationary population of resident cells $v(x,t)$, where both the diffusion and carrying capacity of the invading cells is negatively affected by the resident population.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The nondimensional parameter $\gamma$ in \eqref{eqs:PDEs represents the rate of destruction of resident cells, compared to the proliferation time scale; \citet{browning2019bayesian and \citet{el2021travelling contain further details of this model, including the nondimensionalisation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Initially, the domain consists of a spatially localised invading cell population $u_0(x)$ in a uniform resident cell population $v_0$: \begin{equation}u(x,0) = u_0(x), \qquad v(x,0) = v_0. \label{eq:IC}\end{equation} Note that if $v_0 = 0$ identically, the system \eqref{eqs:PDEs reduces to the Fisher--KPP problem for $u$.}
elements.sent-2411-051: In addition, the diffusivity vanishes when $v=1$; in this paper, we will consider only $0 < v_0 < 1$, as our main focus is on the effects of $v$ on the proliferation, rather than the effect of degenerate diffusion (see the discussion in Sect.~\ref{sec:discussion ).}
elements.sent-2411-052: To simulate \eqref{eqs:PDEs numerically, we discretise in space using a standard cell-centred finite volume method \citep{patankar1980numerical with equally sized cells, and advance in time using the ode15s algorithm in MATLAB.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: To observe the formation of travelling wave behaviour, we consider a large spatial domain $x \in [0, L]$ with $L \gg 1$ approximating a semi-infinite domain, with zero flux conditions ($\partial_x u = 0$) conditions imposed at the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-054: A nonzero initial condition $u(x,0)$ near $x=0$ is then used to represent a localised initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-055: We generally choose $L\sim 150$--$200$ and $N\sim 10^4$ grid points, which is more than adequate for the numerical simulations to have converged.
elements.sent-2411-056: We calculate the wave speed from PDE simulations by estimating the location of the travelling wave front $x_f(t)$ where $u(x_f(t),t) = 0.5$, interpolating the solution between neighbouring grid points if required.
elements.sent-2411-057: This calculation is sensitive in that sufficient time must have passed to minimise the effect of the initial condition, and the domain size $L$ must be sufficiently large to minimise the effect of the right hand boundary.
elements.sent-2411-058: Rather than fit a simple linear function to $x_f$ to estimate the wave speed \citep[e.g.]{el2021travelling , we follow the work of \citet{bramson1983convergence on the Fisher--KPP equation (see also \citet{nolen2017convergence ), who showed that the wave speed is approached only logarithmically as $t\to \infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Correspondingly, we expect that a constant wave speed is approached according to \begin{equation}x_f(t) \sim ct + k_0\log(t) + k_1, \qquad t \to \infty, \label{eq:waveSpeedFit}\end{equation} where $c$ is the wave speed, and $k_0$ and $k_1$ are constants; $k_1$ depends on the initial condition, and while $k_0 = -3/2$ for the minimum-speed Fisher--KPP solution, it likely depends on the parameters for the more complex model \eqref{eqs:PDEs .}
elements.sent-2411-060: To estimate $c$ we thus fit the nonlinear relationship \eqref{eq:waveSpeedFit for large times.}
elements.sent-2411-061: We find that including this logarithmic correction improves the estimate of the long-time wave speed.
elements.sent-2411-062: In Fig.~\ref{fig:PDESolutions we plot typical results (profiles of $u$ and $v$ over time) for a combination of different cell death rates $\gamma$ and initial conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-063: In Fig.~\ref{fig:PDESolutions (a,b), we consider an initially compactly supported invading cell population: \begin{equation}u_0(x) =  1 x < 1 0 x\geq 1, \qquad v_0 = 0.5,\end{equation} for the values $\gamma = 1$ and $\gamma=10$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-064: It is apparent from examining the behaviour of solutions that the system is tending toward a travelling wave of fixed shape and constant speed.
elements.sent-2411-065: Using the method described above, we estimate the wave speed of each of these cases to be $c=1.00$ and $c=1.24$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-066: In Figs.~\ref{fig:PDESolutions (c,d) we consider initial conditions with exponential decay, and larger initial resident cell population: \begin{equation}u_0(x) = \e^{-ax}, \qquad v_0 = 0.75,\end{equation} for $a = 0.27$ and $a = 0.21$, respectively, and again for $\gamma = 1$ and $\gamma = 10$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-067: These decay rates $a$ have been chosen so that \ref{fig:PDESolutions (c) has the same long-time wave speed as \ref{fig:PDESolutions (a), and \ref{fig:PDESolutions (d) has the same long-time wave speed as \ref{fig:PDESolutions (b).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: A linear analysis near the front, as carried out in \citet{el2021travelling , and also below in Sect.~\ref{sec:travWave , predicts that for an exponentially decaying initial condition $u_0(x) = \exp(-ax)$, the wave speed is given by \begin{equation}c = \begin{cases} 2(1-v_0) a \geq 1 \left(a + \frac{1}{a}\right)(1-v_0) a < 1, \end{cases} \label{eq:waveSpeedOnIC}\end{equation} which motivates the choice of initial conditions above (we will see, however, that \eqref{eq:waveSpeedOnIC only holds for sufficiently small $\gamma$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: What is important to note in Fig.~\ref{fig:PDESolutions is that the wave speed is dependent on both the parameter $\gamma$ and the initial conditions of $u$ and $v$, and, indeed, two different sets of initial conditions for the same value of $\gamma$ can result in the same wave speed $c$.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{PDESolutions} \caption{Numerical solutions of the PDE system \eqref{eqs:PDEs}, with various initial conditions and parameter values. In (a, b), $v_0 = 0.5$ and $u_0$ is compactly supported, while $\gamma = 1,10$, respectively. In (c, d), $v=0.75$ and $u_0(x) = \e^{-ax}$ is an exponentially decreasing initial condition, where the value of $a$ has been chosen specifically to result in the same wave speed as for the results in (a) and (b), respectively. The estimated wave speed $c$ (from \eqref{eq:waveSpeedFit}) is noted in each simulation.} \label{fig:PDESolutions}
elements.sent-2411-070: To further explore the possible behaviour of the system \eqref{eqs:PDEs , we run simulations and estimate travelling wave speeds for a much wider range of parameter values and initial conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-071: For compactly supported $u_0(x)$, we estimate the wave speed for values of $\gamma$ ranging from $0.1$ to $10^6$, and for values $v_0 \in \{ 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 \}$.
elements.sent-2411-072: These estimated wave speeds are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedComparison , and will be used to validate the predictions made from the direct computation of travelling wave solutions in the next section.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The most notable property of these speeds is that for sufficiently small $\gamma$, the wave speed is given by $c=2(1-v_0)$ independent of $\gamma$, while for sufficiently large $\gamma$, the wave speed becomes dependent on $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-074: The point of transition between these behaviours depends on $v_0$, but in all cases, as $\gamma\to\infty$, the speed $c$ approaches $2$ from below.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{waveSpeedComparison} \caption{The dependence of wave speed $c$ on death rate $\gamma$, for different values of initial resident cell population $v_0 = V_\infty$. Blue symbols indicate the estimation of wave speed from PDE simulations described in Sect.~\ref{sec:modelAndNumerics}. The dashed curves represent the predicted wave speed according to the condition that $V_\infty = V_c$ \eqref{eq:Vc}, while the solid curves represent the predicted wave speed according to the condition $V_\infty = V_s$, where $V_s$ must be found by numerically solving the system \eqref{eqs:odeSystem}. The PDE simulations with initial condition $v_0 = V_\infty$ predict the same wave speed as the travelling wave analysis.} \label{fig:waveSpeedComparison}
elements.sent-2411-075: We test the effect of initial condition of $u$ by calculating the wave speed for $\gamma \in [0.1, 10^6]$, fixed $v_0 = 0.5$, and exponentially decaying initial conditions $u_0 = \e^{-ax}$ for $a \in \{0.5, 0.325, 0.25\}$.
elements.sent-2411-076: These wave speeds are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedvsIC .}
elements.sent-2411-077: These results show that for sufficiently small $a$ and $\gamma$, the wave speed is given by the formula \eqref{eq:waveSpeedOnIC .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Furthermore, for a fixed $a$ sufficiently small such that the wave speed $c$ is greater than 2, that is \begin{equation}a > a^* = \frac{1}{1-v_0} - \sqrt{\frac{1}{(1-v_0)^2} - 1},\end{equation} the wave speed is given by \eqref{eq:waveSpeedOnIC for any $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The value $a = 0.25$ shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedvsIC satisfies this condition.}
elements.sent-2411-080: On the other hand, if $a < a^*$, for sufficiently large $\gamma$ the wave speed is the same as the $\gamma$-dependent wave speed of the compactly supported initial condition.
elements.sent-2411-081: Equivalently, for a fixed $\gamma$, as $a$ is increased, the wave speed is ultimately limited by the corresponding wave speed of the compactly supported initial condition of the same value of $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-082: In the next section we explain this phenomenon via the calculation of travelling wave solutions in phase space.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{waveSpeedvsIC} \caption{The dependence of wave speed $c$ on death rate $\gamma$, for initial resident cell population $v_0 = 0.5$ and different invading cell initial conditions $u_0 = \exp(-ax)$, for $a \in \{0.5, 0.325, 0.25\}$. The wave speed predicted by the formula \eqref{eq:waveSpeedOnIC} is depicted by the coloured dashed lines. The wave speed of a compactly supported initial condition, corresponding to that in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedComparison}, is shown as black dashed/solid lines.} \label{fig:waveSpeedvsIC}
elements.sent-2411-083: In this section we will compute the travelling wave solutions to \eqref{eqs:PDEs .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Let $z = x-ct$ be the travelling wave coordinate, and let $u(x,t) = U(z)$, $v(x,t) = V(z)$, and $u_x(x,t) = U'(z) = W(z)$.
elements.sent-2411-085: The system of PDEs then becomes the system of three autonomous differential equations: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}U' = W \label{eq:Ueq} V' = \frac{\gamma }{c} UV \label{eq:Veq} W' = \frac{1}{1-V}\left(\frac{\gamma}{c} UVW - cW - U(1-U-V) \right). \label{eq:Weq}\end{equation} \end{subequations This system \eqref{eqs:odeSystem has an isolated equilibrium at $(U,V,W) = (1,0,0)$.}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}U' = W \label{eq:Ueq} V' = \frac{\gamma }{c} UV \label{eq:Veq} W' = \frac{1}{1-V}\left(\frac{\gamma}{c} UVW - cW - U(1-U-V) \right). \label{eq:Weq}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-086: In addition, each point on the $V$-axis $(U,V,W) = (0,V_\infty,0)$ is an equilbrium, each of which is therefore not isolated.
elements.sent-2411-087: Travelling wave solutions correspond to heteroclinic orbits connecting $(1,0,0)$ (as $z\to-\infty$) to a point on the $V$-axis (as $z\to\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The travelling wave solution relevant to an initial value problem to \eqref{eqs:PDEs with $v(x,0) = v_0$ is one in which this point on the $V$-axis has $V_\infty=v_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The eigenvalues and eigenvectors of $(1,0,0)$ are \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\lambda_1 = \frac{\gamma}{c}, \quad \lambda_2 = \frac{-c + \sqrt{c^2 + 4}}{2}, \quad \lambda_3 = \frac{-c - \sqrt{c^2 + 4}}{2}.\end{equation}. \begin{equation}\bm E_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 1 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \bm E_{2,3} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 0 \lambda_{2,3} \end{bmatrix}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-090: At the other end, the eigenvalues near a point $(0,V_\infty,0)$ are \begin{equation}\lambda_1' = 0, \quad \lambda_2' = -\mathcal C + \sqrt{\mathcal C^2 - 1}, \qquad \lambda_3' = -\mathcal C - \sqrt{\mathcal C^2 - 1}, \quad \mathcal C = \frac{c}{2(1-V_\infty)}, \label{eq:eigsAtInfinity}\end{equation} with eigenvectors \begin{equation}\bm E_1' =  0 1 0 , \quad \bm E'_{2,3} =  \lambda'_{2,3} \gamma V_\infty/ c (\lambda'_{2,3})^2 .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-091: The zero eigenvalue $\lambda_1'$ (with eigenvector pointing along the $V$-axis) is due to the non-isolated nature of each fixed point.
elements.sent-2411-092: The other two eigenvalues are negative, indicating that trajectories are attracted to the $V$-axis in its neighbourhood.
elements.sent-2411-093: As there is one degree of freedom in trajectories leaving $(1,0,0)$, there is a one-parameter family of trajectories that connect $(1,0,0)$ to the $V$-axis, each ending at a different value of $V_\infty$ between $0$ and $1$.
elements.sent-2411-094: In \citet{colson2021travelling trajectories of their similar system were found via a shooting algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Here we compute solutions of \eqref{eqs:odeSystem numerically by solving it as a boundary value problem on a large domain $z \in (0, z_\infty)$, using the numerical continuation package Auto-07p \citep{doedel2007auto .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: This will allow us to compute solutions for more extreme values of parameters (in particular $\gamma$).
elements.sent-2411-097: Appropriate boundary conditions are \begin{equation}U(0) = 1 + \varepsilon, \quad W(0) = -\epsilon\lambda_2, \quad V(z_\infty) = V_\infty\end{equation} where $\varepsilon$ is a small parameter, and $z_\infty \gg 1$.
elements.sent-2411-098: These boundary conditions enforce that near $(1,0,0)$, trajectories are on the eigenvector with the smaller positive eigenvalue.
elements.sent-2411-099: For a given $\gamma$ and $c$ we are able to construct travelling wave solutions for any value of $V_\infty$ between 0 and 1, by starting at $V_\infty=0$ (at which point $V$ vanishes identically, and $U,W$ are solutions to the Fisher--KPP system), and then increasing $V_\infty$ by numerical continuation.
elements.sent-2411-100: We plot examples of such trajectories, projected onto the $(U,V)$ plane, in Fig.~\ref{fig:phasePlanes .}
elements.sent-2411-101: In this figure we choose two sets of parameters $(\gamma, c) = (1,1)$ and $(10, 1.24)$ in order to compare to the travelling wave solutions observed in the PDE simulations shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:PDESolutions .}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{phasePlanes} \caption{Trajectories in $(U,V,W)$ phase space, projected onto the $(U,V)$ plane, for (a) $(\gamma, c) = (1,1)$ and (b) $(\gamma, c) = (10, 1.24)$. In (a), the parameters are such that trajectories spiral into the $V$-axis for $V < V_c$, and monotonically approach the $V$-axis for $V \geq V_c$. In (b), there is also a critical value $V_s$ such that $U$ approaches zero from below when $V < V_s$ and above when $V > V_s$. Dotted curves marked (a)--(d), corresponding to the late-time PDE simulations depicted in Figs.~\ref{fig:PDESolutions}a--d, respectively, show that PDE simulations are attracted to travelling wave solutions with either the same decay rate as initial condition (if such a solution exists), or to the travelling wave with fastest decay rate, at $V_\infty = \max\{V_c, V_s\}$. } \label{fig:phasePlanes}
elements.sent-2411-102: For given values of $\gamma$ and $c$, there is a one-parameter family of travelling wave solutions, that we may consider as characterised by the value $V_\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-103: Since the wave speed $c$ is a parameter of the system, we thus expect that for a given $V_\infty$ there is a one-parameter family of travelling wave solutions, parametrised by $c$, and it is not yet apparent which travelling wave solution will correspond to the long-time behaviour of a solution to the PDE system \eqref{eqs:PDEs for a given initial condition.}
elements.sent-2411-104: In order to determine this wavespeed selection, we need to consider the behaviour of trajectories near the front of the travelling wave, that is, near the $V$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-105: Firstly, we note that for a given wavespeed $c$, there is a critical value $V = V_c$, at which the eigenvalues $\lambda'_{2,3}$ switch from complex to real: \begin{equation}V_c = 1 - \frac{c}{2}. \label{eq:Vc}\end{equation} This value is independent of the decay rate $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-106: Trajectories for which $V_\infty < V_c$ spiral into the relevant point on the $V$-axis, and since this corresponds to negative values of $U$, such trajectories cannot correspond to solutions to \eqref{eqs:PDEs that have evolved from a non-negative initial condition.}
elements.sent-2411-107: This property is determined locally, and is equivalent to the condition used to determine the minimum wave speed in the Fisher--KPP equation.
elements.sent-2411-108: However, it is not always the case that $U$ is positive on trajectories for which $V_\infty > V_c$.
elements.sent-2411-109: This is true in particular when $\gamma$ is sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-110: In Fig.~\ref{fig:phasePlanes (b) (in particular the inset of that figure) in which $\gamma=10$, we observe that trajectories for which $V_\infty$ is slightly larger than $V_c$ still approach the $V$-axis from the negative $(U<0)$ side, despite the fact that eigenvalues are real at these points.}
elements.sent-2411-111: In this parameter regime there is a point $V_s$ (indicated as a red dot in Fig.~\ref{fig:phasePlanes (b)) that separates trajectories that have $U$ become negative, from those that remain positive.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The existence of such a threshold value was also noted in the similar model of \citet{colson2021travelling .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Unlike the point $V_c$, the point $V_s$ is not determined as a local property of the phase space (it is a property of the manner in which the unstable manifold of $(1,0,0)$ intersects the $V$-axis), so is unlikely to have a closed-form expression.
elements.sent-2411-114: We may understand the existence of this critical point $V_s$ by considering the linearisation near the $V$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-115: For $V_c < V_\infty < 1$, a trajectory near the $V$-axis will behave as \begin{equation}\begin{bmatrix} U V W \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix}0 V_\infty 0 \end{bmatrix} + r_2 \bm E_2'\e^{\lambda_2' z} + r_3 \bm E_3'\e^{\lambda_3' z}, \qquad z\to\infty, \label{eq:nearVaxisExpansion}\end{equation} where $\lambda_{2,3 '$ and $\bm E_{2,3 '$ are the eigenvalues and eigenvectors \eqref{eq:eigsAtInfinity .}}}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation} U V W  \sim 0 V_\infty 0  + r_2 \bm E_2'\e^{\lambda_2' z} + r_3 \bm E_3'\e^{\lambda_3' z}, \qquad z\to\infty, \label{eq:nearVaxisExpansion}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-116: For $V>V_c$ , $\lambda_3' < \lambda_2' < 0$, so that the generic case is that $U\to 0$ with decay rate given by the less negative eigenvector $\lambda_2'$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The magnitudes of one of the constants $r_2$ and $r_3$ is arbitrary due to translational invariance, but these constants are otherwise unique properties of a given trajectory.
elements.sent-2411-118: Importantly, a trajectory may have $r_2 > 0$, which means that (for the eigenvector written as in \eqref{eq:eigsAtInfinity ) the trajectory approaches the $V$-axis from the negative $U$ direction.}
elements.sent-2411-119: These are the trajectories observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:phasePlanes (b) for $V_c < V_\infty < V_s$.}
elements.sent-2411-120: These trajectories also cannot correspond to a travelling wave solution of \eqref{eqs:PDEs reached from a non-negative initial condition.}
elements.sent-2411-121: The critical point $V_s$ represents the end point of the particular trajectory at which the coefficient $r_2$ in \eqref{eq:nearVaxisExpansion goes from being positive to negative, that is, at $V_\infty = V_s$, $r_2 = 0$.}
elements.sent-2411-122: What is special about this point then is that $U\to 0$ with decay rate given by the more negative eigenvalue $\lambda_3'$.
elements.sent-2411-123: In other words, for parameter values in which a point $V_s$ exists, the trajectory ending at $V_s$ (rather than $V_c$) is the travelling wave solution with the greatest decay as $z\to \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Trajectories that end at $V_\infty > \max\{V_c, V_s\}$ are also valid travelling wave solutions that exhibit a specific exponential decay.
elements.sent-2411-125: Indeed, when we plot the curves in the $(U,V)$ plane that correspond to the late-time PDE simulations (see Fig.~\ref{fig:phasePlanes ), we see that these exactly correspond to trajectories on the phase plane, terminating either at $V_c$ or $V_s$ (for the compactly supported initial conditions), or at a higher value (for the slowly exponentially decaying initial condition).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Identifying the eigenvalue $\lambda_2'$ from \eqref{eq:eigsAtInfinity with $-a$, a travelling wave solution will have decay rate $a$ given that the wave speed \eqref{eq:waveSpeedOnIC holds (which is how that formula is derived).}}
elements.sent-2411-127: However, as observed in the PDE simulations depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedvsIC , for sufficiently large $a$, the wave speed is replaced by that corresponding to a compactly supported initial condition.}
elements.sent-2411-128: This is because a trajectory may only end at a given $V_\infty$ when $V_\infty > \max\{V_c, V_s\}$ for the given wave speed and value of $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-129: For sufficiently large $a$, the wave speed will be limited either by the existence of $V_c$, so that \begin{equation}c = 2(1-V_\infty),\end{equation} or by the existence of $V_s$, in which case $c$ will be the value such that $v_0 = V_s$ for given $(\gamma, c)$.
elements.sent-2411-130: We calculate the location of $V_s$ in our numerical continuation procedure by tracking the value of $U(z_\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-131: While in the limit $z_\infty\to\infty$ this value will vanish, for a finite $z_\infty < \infty$ it is generally small but nonzero.
elements.sent-2411-132: In this case, the value of $V_\infty$ where $U(z_\infty) = 0$ corresponds (closely enough for the computations) to the threshold value $V_\infty = V_s$.
elements.sent-2411-133: Using numerical continuation we are then able to fix $U(z_\infty)=0$ and allow $\gamma$ and $c$ to vary, for a given $V_\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-134: The parameter $\gamma$ can then be increased to very large values, for which the wave speed slowly approaches $2$.
elements.sent-2411-135: As $\gamma$ is decreased, eventually $V_s$ collides with the other critical point $V_c$.
elements.sent-2411-136: Using this procedure we produce curves of wave speed $c$ as a function of $\gamma$, for given values of the far-field resident cell population $V_\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-137: These results are plotted in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedComparison along with the estimates from the PDE simulations for compactly supported initial conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-138: When $\gamma$ is sufficiently small such that $V_s$ does not exist, we instead use the condition that $V_\infty = V_c$, thus $c = 2(1-V_\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-139: The comparison confirms that we have correctly identified the wave speed selection mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-140: As we can explicitly calculate travelling wave solutions, we are able to continue to much larger values of $\gamma$ than would be feasible if we relied only on PDE simulations.
elements.sent-2411-141: We observe in Fig.~\ref{fig:waveSpeedComparison that in the limit that $\gamma$ becomes large, the wave speed tends to a constant value $2$, but does so very slowly.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Example travelling wave profiles are shown for $V_\infty = 0.5$, and $\gamma \in\{10, 10^5, 10^{11}\}$, in Fig.~\ref{fig:bigGammaProfiles .}
elements.sent-2411-143: These profiles highlight that as $\gamma$ increases, the travelling wave solutions exhibit a region in which both cell populations are very small, and this region grows, but again slowly, as $\gamma\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-144: This region corresponds to the hypocellular interstitial gap described in \citet{gatenby1996reaction .}
elements.sent-2411-145: In the next section we carry out the matched asymptotic analysis in this limit, which will explain the logarithmic dependence on $\gamma$ of both the wave speed and the interstitial gap width.
elements.sent-2411-146: In this section we describe the asymptotic analysis of the relation between resident cell death rate $\gamma$ and wave speed $c$ in the limit that $\gamma$ becomes large.
elements.sent-2411-147: We restrict ourselves to the travelling wave branches that correspond to compactly supported initial conditions (that is, $V_\infty = V_s$ in the preceding section).
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{bigGammaProfiles} \caption{Travelling wave solutions for $U$ (blue solid curves) and $V$ (red dashed curves), for $V_\infty = 0.5$ and (a) $\gamma = 10$, (b) $\gamma = 10^5$, and (c) $\gamma = 10^{11}$. The interstitial gap (the region in which both cell populations are small) is seen to grow slowly; the asymptotic analysis of Section \ref{sec:asymptotics} predicts this region is of order $\log\gamma$ as $\gamma\to\infty$.} \label{fig:bigGammaProfiles}
elements.sent-2411-148: The important observation is that for large $\gamma$, \eqref{eq:Veq implies that $V$ is asymptotically small except near the front (where $V\to V_\infty$ as $z\to\infty$).}
elements.sent-2411-149: The effect of $\gamma$ is thus to modify the leading order, Fisher--KPP solution, a property that was observed in PDE simulations in \citet{el2021travelling .}
elements.sent-2411-150: In addition, this phenomenon is very similar to that which occurs when the Fisher--KPP system is modified by a small cut-off factor in the reaction term \eqref{eq:fkppCutoff , as considered in \citet{brunet1997shift and \citet{dumortier2007critical .}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: Our asymptotic analysis will follow along similar lines to \citet{brunet1997shift .}
elements.sent-2411-152: We will also numerically determine the prefactors in the leading order asymptotic approximations, which are required to observe the effect of $V_\infty$ on the asymptotic wave speed.
elements.sent-2411-153: Let $\gamma \gg 1$ and let the wave speed $c = 2 - \delta$, where $\delta \to 0 $ as $\gamma \to \infty$ is the correction to the wave speed.
elements.sent-2411-154: In the limit $\gamma \gg 1$, \eqref{eq:Veq implies that $V=0$ to leading order, so that to leading order, $U$ and $W$ are given by travelling wave solutions to the Fisher--KPP system \eqref{eq:fkpp with wavespeed $c = 2$: \begin{equation}U \sim U_0(z), \qquad W \sim W_0(z), \qquad \delta\to 0, \qquad z-z_0 = O(1)\end{equation} where \begin{equation}U_0' = W_0, \qquad W_0' = -2W_0 - U_0(1-U_0), \qquad \lim_{z\to-\infty} (U_0, W_0) = (1,0). \label{eq:FisherKppSystem}\end{equation} As $z\to \infty$, the behaviour of this leading order solution is determined by the repeated eigenvalue of the Fisher--KPP system near the origin: \begin{equation}U_0 \sim A_0(z-z_0)\e^{-(z-z_0)}, \qquad z\to \infty. \label{eq:U0AsymptoticBehaviour}\end{equation} In \eqref{eq:U0AsymptoticBehaviour we have explicitly written the arbitrary constant of translation $z_0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: In this form the prefactor $A_0$ is not arbitrary, but is a constant that is a property of the unique solution to \eqref{eq:FisherKppSystem .}
elements.sent-2411-156: The numerical value of $A_0$ may be found by computing the value of \begin{equation}\log(A_0) = \lim_{z\to\infty} \ell(z), \qquad \ell(z) = \frac{U_0}{U_0+W_0} + \log|U_0+W_0|. \label{eq:ell}\end{equation} By solving \eqref{eq:FisherKppSystem numerically and using the formula \eqref{eq:ell , we determine that \begin{equation}A_0 \approx 0.1419.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: As $z$ increases, the system \eqref{eqs:odeSystem reaches an intermediate region in which the effect of subcritical wave speed ($\delta > 0$) becomes important.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Since this effect is to reduce the wave speed below the critical value $2$, the leading order Fisher--KPP approximation is pushed into the regime in which the origin is a stable spiral.
elements.sent-2411-159: That is, \begin{equation}U \sim U_M, \qquad (2-\delta)U_M' + 2U_M'' + U_M,\end{equation} with solution (matching to the outer solution \eqref{eq:U0AsymptoticBehaviour ) \begin{equation}U_M = A_0\delta^{-1/2} \e^{-(z-z_0)} \sin\left(\delta^{1/2}(z-z_0)\right). \label{eq:UMAsymptoticBehaviour}\end{equation} This asymptotic behaviour holds when $\delta \to 0$ with $z-z_0 = O(\delta^{-1/2 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: It is this intermediate behaviour that must be matched to the inner region in which $V$ is no longer small.
elements.sent-2411-161: In the inner region, $U$ and $W$ are small, and $V = O(1)$.
elements.sent-2411-163: To leading order in this inner region, the system \eqref{eqs:odeSystem is \begin{equation}\hat U' = \hat W, \quad V' = \frac{1}{2}\hat U V, \quad \hat W' = \frac{1}{1-V}\left(\frac{1}{2}\hat U V\hat W - 2\hat W - \hat U(1-V)\right). \label{eq:innerProblem}\end{equation} Considering solutions for which $V_\infty = V_s$, the relevant inner boundary condition is that the trajectory approaches the given $V=V_\infty$ along the more negative eigenvector, that is, \begin{equation}\begin{bmatrix} \hat U V \hat W \end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix} 0 V_\infty 0\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} \Lambda V_\infty/2 \Lambda^2 \end{bmatrix} \e^{\Lambda z}, \quad z\to\infty, \label{eq:innerProblemIC}\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\Lambda = -\frac{1}{1-V_\infty}\left(1 + \sqrt{1 - (1-V_\infty)^2}\right)\end{equation} is the leading order ($c\to 2$) eigenvalue corresponding to $\lambda_3'$ in \eqref{eq:eigsAtInfinity .}}
elements.formula-2411-029: \begin{equation}\hat U' = \hat W, \quad V' = \frac{1}{2}\hat U V, \quad \hat W' = \frac{1}{1-V}\left(\frac{1}{2}\hat U V\hat W - 2\hat W - \hat U(1-V)\right). \label{eq:innerProblem}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-030: \begin{equation} \hat U V \hat W  \sim  0 V_\infty 0 +  \Lambda V_\infty/2 \Lambda^2  \e^{\Lambda z}, \quad z\to\infty, \label{eq:innerProblemIC}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-164: This inner problem is too intractable to solve in closed form.
elements.sent-2411-165: However, we only require the far field ($z \to -\infty$) behaviour of these solutions to match to the intermediate region \eqref{eq:UMAsymptoticBehaviour .}
elements.sent-2411-166: As $z\to -\infty$, $V\to 0$ and the leading order inner problem \eqref{eq:innerProblem approaches the linearised version of the critical-speed Fisher--KPP equation near the origin, that is \begin{equation}\hat W' \sim -2\hat W - \hat U, \qquad \hat U' = \hat W, \qquad z \to -\infty.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-032: \begin{equation}\hat W' \sim -2\hat W - \hat U, \qquad \hat U' = \hat W, \qquad z \to -\infty.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-167: Thus, similar to the outer problem, \begin{equation}\hat U \sim -A_I(z-z_I)\e^{-(z-z_I)}, \qquad z\to -\infty. \label{eq:UIAsymptoticBehaviour}\end{equation} Again $z_I$ is arbitrary due to the translational invariance of \eqref{eq:innerProblem , but the constant $A_I $ depends in a nontrivial way on the entire solution to \eqref{eq:innerProblem , and thus on $V_\infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: With the sign convention in \eqref{eq:UIAsymptoticBehaviour , $A_I$ will be positive.}
elements.sent-2411-169: By integrating $\eqref{eq:innerProblem}$ numerically in the negative $z$ direction, from an initial condition \eqref{eq:innerProblemIC , we may numerically compute $A_I$ independently of $z_0$ in a similar way as to the Fisher--KPP system, by computing \begin{equation}\log(A_I) = \lim_{z\to -\infty} \frac{\hat U}{\hat U+\hat W} + \log|\hat U+\hat W|.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-170: For the typical values of $V_\infty \in \{0.25, 0.5, 0.75\}$ we have considered thus far, we use this expression to estimate \begin{equation}\left.A_I\right|_{V_\infty = 0.25} \approx 0.515, \qquad \left.A_I\right|_{V_\infty = 0.5} \approx 1.485, \qquad \left.A_I\right|_{V_\infty = 0.75} \approx 2.943.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-171: In order for the inner solution \eqref{eq:UIAsymptoticBehaviour to match to the intermediate region \eqref{eq:UMAsymptoticBehaviour , the sinusoidal term in the intermediate solution must approach zero, which happens when its argument is in the neighbourhood of $\pi$.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: We define a new spatial variable $\xi$ by $z - z_0 = \delta^{-1/2}\pi + \xi$.
elements.sent-2411-173: In matching we then require $z_I = z_0 + \delta^{-1/2}\pi$, and \begin{equation}-A_0\e^{-\pi\delta^{-1/2}}\xi\e^{-\xi} \sim -A_I\gamma^{-1}\xi\e^{-\xi},\end{equation} from which we obtain the relationship between the wave speed correction $\delta$ and $\gamma$: \begin{equation}\delta \sim \pi^2\left[\log\left(\frac{A_0}{A_I}\gamma\right)\right]^{-2} \sim \frac{\pi^2}{[\log\gamma]^2} + \frac{2\pi^2\log(A_I/A_0)}{[\log\gamma]^3}. \label{eq:asymptoticDelta}\end{equation} The first term on the right hand side of \eqref{eq:asymptoticDelta is equivalent to the universal correction that arises in the Fisher--KPP equation with cut-off \citep{brunet1997shift,dumortier2007critical .}}
elements.sent-2411-174: It does not depend on the specific value of $V_\infty$, and indeed we expect it would not depend on the specifics of the diffusion or reaction terms being considered, as it is purely a consequence of the fact that the intermediate problem has to match to an inner problem where $U$ is of order $\gamma^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-175: In Fig.~\ref{fig:asymptoticWaveSpeed a we present numerical evidence that this leading order term is correct, by plotting $(\log\gamma)^{-2}$ against the correction to the velocity $\delta = 2-c$, for the travelling wave branches we have computed for each of $V_\infty \in\{ 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 \}$.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Each of these curves is seen to approach the line with slope $\pi^2$, as predicted by the leading order term in \eqref{eq:asymptoticDelta .}
elements.sent-2411-177: The convergence to this limit is very slow, which is unsurprising given that the asymptotic series is logarithmic in $\gamma$.
elements.sent-2411-178: To test the correction term (the second term on the right hand side of \eqref{eq:asymptoticDelta ) we compute the difference between the velocity correction and the leading order term, that is \begin{equation}\delta_1 = 2 - c - \frac{\pi^2}{[\log\gamma]^2},\end{equation} and, in Fig.~\ref{fig:asymptoticWaveSpeed b, plot this quantity as a function of $(\log\gamma)^{-2}$, along with the asymptotic prediction.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: The asymptotic prediction depends on $V_\infty$ through $A_I$, so is different for each branch.
elements.sent-2411-180: This comparison confirms that the correction term improves the estimate in the limit $\gamma\to\infty$, but again highlights the very slow convergence of the asymptotic approximation in this limit.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=.85\textwidth]{asymptoticWaveSpeed} \caption{Comparison between the numerically determined travelling wave speeds and the asymptotic approximation for large $\gamma$. (a) Comparison between the wavespeed correction $\delta = 2-c$ and numerically determined values and the leading order term in \eqref{eq:asymptoticDelta} show convergence to the leading order universal logarithmic behaviour that is independent of $V_\infty$. (b) Comparison between the next term in the asymptotic approximation \eqref{eq:asymptoticDelta}, in which the effect of $V_\infty$ plays a role.} \label{fig:asymptoticWaveSpeed}
elements.sent-2411-181: Finally, we note that in the intermediate region in which $U$ behaves as \eqref{eq:UMAsymptoticBehaviour , both $U$ and $V$ are small.}
elements.sent-2411-182: This region therefore corresponds to the interstitial region that can be observed in Fig.~\ref{fig:bigGammaProfiles .}
elements.sent-2411-183: The asymptotic analysis predicts that the width of this region is $O(\delta^{-1/2 ) = O(\log\gamma)$, hence the slow growth of this region in the large $\gamma$ limit.}
elements.sent-2411-184: In this article we have determined the properties of the three dimensional phase space that select the wave speed of the system \eqref{eqs:PDEs for compactly supported or exponentially decaying initial conditions.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Although the phase-space properties of the three-dimensional system in travelling wave coordinates \eqref{eqs:odeSystem are more complicated than the two-dimensional Fisher--KPP system, the general principle of wave speed selection is the same.}
elements.sent-2411-186: For an initial condition of given far field exponential decay, a PDE solution will evolve toward the travelling wave solution which has the same decay rate, if such a nonnegative solution exists.
elements.sent-2411-187: Otherwise, the solution will tend to the travelling wave solution which has the largest decay rate.
elements.sent-2411-188: Depending on the value of $\gamma$ and $v_0$, the solution with fastest decay rate will either correspond to the wave speed $c$ such that $v_0 = V_c$, or such that $v_0 = V_s$, if that point exists.
elements.sent-2411-189: An important point regarding the determination of $V_s$ is, although it is a property of the phase space, it is not a local property in the neighbourhood of the $V$-axis; rather, it is related to the manner in which the two-dimensional unstable manifold of $(U,V,W) = (1,0,0)$ intersects the $V$-axis.
elements.sent-2411-190: For this reason, the existence or location of $V_s$ cannot be determined by a local expansion, so there is unlikely to be a general closed-form expression for the selected wave speed.
elements.sent-2411-191: We have also performed the asymptotic analysis of travelling waves in the large $\gamma$ limit.
elements.sent-2411-192: In carrying out this limit it is useful to think of the system as a generalisation of the Fisher--KPP system in which the reaction term is perturbed, leading to a logarithmically small correction to the wave speed from the critical Fisher--KPP wave speed of $2$.
elements.sent-2411-193: The leading order correction in this case is universal, so is likely to be the correction observed for a large class of systems that look like \eqref{eqs:PDEs with variations in diffusivity and reaction terms; for example, the system considered by \citet{colson2021travelling , in which the resident cell population effects the diffusivity but not the reaction, is likely to have the same leading order asymptotic behaviour.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: In this work we have not considered the degenerate-diffusion case where $v_0 = 1$ (or $V_\infty = 1$ in calculating travelling wave solutions).
elements.sent-2411-195: In this case, as discussed in \citet{el2021travelling and in the closely related problem in \citet{colson2021travelling , the appropriate trajectories end at the point $(U,V,W) = (0,1,0)$, which is a removable singularity in the phase space (as it is the intersection of the $V$-axis and the plane $V=1$), rather than a fixed point.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: Travelling waves therefore do not behave exponentially near this point.
elements.sent-2411-197: A local expansion will give the permitted behaviour near this point; one method of recovering this is to desingularise the system \eqref{eqs:odeSystem and perform a centre manifold analysis, similar to that considered in \citet{gallay2022propagation , for example.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: It is unclear if, in this case, the selected wave speed $c$ (corresponding to a compactly supported initial condition) will be selected by a mechanism similar to that which determines the point $V_s$: that is, out of all trajectories over $c$ that end at $(0,1,0)$, there will be a unique trajectory such that the decay is maximum.
elements.sent-2411-199: We note that the PDE simulations in \citet{el2021travelling suggest that the wavespeed at $v_0=1$ is also the one approached if one takes the limit as $v_0\to 1$ from below, which indicates that the wave speed selection mechanism should be related.}
